note: This is not the deterministic simulator. Each panel row is an LLM call instructed to respond as one synthetic person/persona; aggregation is computed after those responses.

ideas.summary: Convert screenshots, memes, and reaction images into motion clips, voiceovers, remix chains, and export-ready TikTok/Reels/X posts.
Make a pet into a recurring character with consistent voice, expressions, episodic plots, captions, skit templates, and merch mockups.
Optimize dating profiles with photo sequencing, prompt rewrites, opener suggestions, voice-note practice, mock conversations, and authenticity-first exports.
Transform family photos, voice notes, and text into narrated mini-documentaries, timelines, gentle restorations, videos, books, and family-sharing artifacts.
Transcribe voice journals into themes, progress arcs, weekly recaps, safety-aware reflection prompts, and optional polished share cards.
Convert couple questionnaires, voice notes, and photos into a playful game-show-style compatibility recap with private sharing and event packs.
Put the learner into visual roleplay scenes and practice everyday scenarios with AI voice, corrections, cues, and travel packs.
Turn a product link, a few photos, and a creator/persona choice into native-looking short-video ads for sellers, creators, resellers, and DTC micro-brands.
Turn selected group-chat moments, photos, polls, and weekend highlights into a recurring magazine cover and recap issue.
Turn streaks and habits into weekly montage recaps, proof cards, friend competitions, group leaderboards, and milestone celebration clips.

personas.name: Maya
personas.context: Runs a small TikTok Shop side hustle, makes product clips after work, watches creator economy YouTube, pays for tools if they save time.
personas.budget: $20-80/mo for tools that earn back money
personas.habits: TikTok, CapCut, Shopify, Instagram Reels, group chats with other sellers
personas.skepticism: Hates generic AI ads, worries about ad policy and fake-looking people.

personas.name: Jules
personas.context: College student, posts outfit and friend-group content, uses AI filters casually but deletes anything that feels cringe.
personas.habits: TikTok search, Instagram Stories, Pinterest, Snapchat, iMessage
personas.skepticism: Won't pay unless the output makes her look cooler or saves embarrassment.

personas.name: Marco
personas.context: Single professional, tired of dating apps, wants better photos/prompts but does not want to feel fake.
personas.budget: $30-150 one-time if it clearly improves matches
personas.habits: Hinge, Instagram, group texts, gym, local events
personas.skepticism: Distrusts over-edited photos and anything that makes him sound like a pickup artist.

personas.name: Tanya
personas.context: Parent with thousands of photos, shares privately with family, buys photo books before holidays.
personas.habits: iMessage, Facebook groups, Instagram, Google Photos, school parent chats
personas.skepticism: Child privacy and weird AI faces are dealbreakers.

personas.name: Devon
personas.context: Lives in group chats and fandom/meme communities, makes jokes fast, wants tools that produce funny shareable artifacts in seconds.
personas.habits: X, Discord, TikTok, Reddit, Instagram DMs
personas.skepticism: If the joke takes too long or looks branded, it dies.

personas.name: Nina
personas.context: Tracks workouts and runs, likes accountability challenges, shares progress but avoids performative hustle culture.
personas.habits: Strava, Apple Health, Instagram Stories, gym group chat
personas.skepticism: Does not want another streak app that nags her and then becomes guiltware.

personas.name: Aarav
personas.context: Travels for work, wants practical language confidence and would practice if scenarios felt real.
personas.habits: YouTube, Duolingo, WhatsApp, Google Maps, travel TikTok
personas.skepticism: Hates classroom-feeling exercises and robotic voice practice.

personas.name: Bianca
personas.context: Dog owner, posts pet clips weekly, buys silly pet merch, sends pet videos constantly.
personas.habits: TikTok, Instagram Reels, family group chat, Chewy
personas.skepticism: Novelty fades unless her dog becomes a repeat character.

personas.name: Owen
personas.context: Makes a podcast/newsletter on nights and weekends, wants clips and assets but is allergic to complicated workflows.
personas.skepticism: Will churn if the tool makes bland content that looks like everyone else's.

personas.name: Sasha
personas.context: Saves outfits, rooms, recipes, and products constantly, wants help making sense of screenshots and finding cheaper alternatives.
personas.habits: TikTok search, Pinterest, Instagram saves, Amazon, resale apps
personas.skepticism: Does not trust tools that push sponsored junk or bad matches.

personas.name: Leo
personas.context: Uses AI for studying and jokes, likes competitive mini-games, shares quiz results and funny cards with friends.
personas.habits: Discord, TikTok, Snapchat, Canvas, group chats
personas.skepticism: If it feels like homework, he bounces.

personas.name: Rachel
personas.context: Busy household decision-maker, cooks at home, redecorates slowly, wants practical ideas that look good enough to share.
personas.habits: Instagram, Pinterest, grocery apps, home decor TikTok, family texts
personas.skepticism: Unrealistic AI outputs annoy her. She needs doable, budget-aware help.

ranked_reactions.price_ceiling: I would do maybe $12-15 a month during heavy use, or happily pay $79-149 for a really good finished gift product.

ranked_reactions.trigger_to_try: If it had a tasteful anniversary pack that made a funny private recap video or printable keepsake in under 15 minutes, I would consider it.
ranked_reactions.price_ceiling: Maybe $10-20 one time for an occasion, but I would not subscribe.
ranked_reactions.quote: I would do this once if it was more cute than creepy.

ranked_reactions.why_it_hits: As a busy parent, voice is easier than typing, so I do understand the appeal of talking through stress or life logistics while driving or cleaning up. The recap part could help me notice patterns instead of just mentally spinning.
ranked_reactions.biggest_objection: My private thoughts are not something I want floating around an AI app, and I do not want fake wellness language talking back to me.
ranked_reactions.trigger_to_try: If it had strong privacy controls, local processing, and very practical summaries instead of therapy-speak, I might test it.
ranked_reactions.price_ceiling: Maybe $5-8 a month if I trusted it, but honestly this is not a priority spend for me.
ranked_reactions.quote: I like the idea of speaking instead of typing, but this would have to feel very private and very no-nonsense.

ignored_or_rejected.reason: This feels made for people posting constantly on TikTok or Reels, which is not me. I share family stuff privately, and I do not need more meme tools.
Even if it is funny, it is still a novelty app to me. I would maybe laugh at someone else's pet video, but I would not pay to build a whole pet character universe.
I am married, so this is just irrelevant to my life.
I am not looking for language learning right now, and the selfie roleplay part sounds awkward more than helpful.
I am not selling products, and AI-generated ad people immediately make me think of fake content.
I do not want one more app turning basic habits into performative scorekeeping. It feels tiring, not motivating.

parsed.most_viral_artifact: A short private family year-in-review video or mini digital scrapbook from Family Lore Studio that I would text to grandparents and maybe post selectively to a private Facebook group.
parsed.trust_breaker: If the app reused my kids' photos to train models, made their faces look uncanny, auto-shared anything publicly, or mixed up details and basically fabricated family memories, I would delete it immediately.
parsed.surprise: I am actually more willing to pay a fairly high one-time price for a polished family keepsake than a lower monthly subscription, because I think in terms of holiday gifts and grandparent sharing, not 'content creation tools.'

ranked_reactions.price_ceiling: I could see paying around $8-12 a month if I used it every week and the summaries were actually insightful.
ranked_reactions.quote: If this helps me notice when I am improving or overtraining without turning into guiltware, I am in.

ranked_reactions.biggest_objection: It could get invasive fast if it starts scraping chat moments people did not mean to immortalize, or if the humor feels AI-generic instead of actually our vibe.
ranked_reactions.trigger_to_try: If one person in my group could make a recap from selected messages and photos only, and everyone could react to it without downloading another app, I would try it.
ranked_reactions.price_ceiling: I would maybe pay $3-5 occasionally for a really good recap, but I do not want another subscription for this.
ranked_reactions.quote: This is cute if it feels like our group made it, not like AI turned our chat into a corny newsletter.

ranked_reactions.biggest_objection: This is one tiny step away from becoming exactly the kind of streak app I hate, where if I miss three days the app starts making me feel bad.
ranked_reactions.trigger_to_try: If it auto-made a tasteful weekly progress reel from Strava and Apple Health without pushing competitions by default, I would test it.
ranked_reactions.price_ceiling: Maybe $5-8 a month if it stayed lightweight and did not spam me with nudges.
ranked_reactions.quote: I want the satisfying progress video, not a digital coach yelling at me for missing leg day.

ranked_reactions.biggest_objection: I would worry it would flatten my personality into the same hyper-optimized dating profile as everyone else.
ranked_reactions.trigger_to_try: If a friend said it gave actually specific feedback tied to my photos instead of generic dating advice, I would try it before redoing a profile.
ranked_reactions.price_ceiling: I would do a one-time $10-15 thing before I would subscribe.

ranked_reactions.biggest_objection: I would be really sensitive to it hallucinating details or making my family memories feel overly sentimental and fake.

ranked_reactions.biggest_objection: This feels like a creativity toy more than a need, and I would get embarrassed fast if the output looked overproduced or try-hard.
ranked_reactions.trigger_to_try: If it could turn one screenshot plus one running joke into a polished Story in 30 seconds, I would mess with it.
ranked_reactions.price_ceiling: Free is ideal; maybe a couple bucks one time for exports, but I would not subscribe.
ranked_reactions.quote: Funny for a post-race meme, but I am not trying to become a content factory.

ranked_reactions.why_it_hits: I can imagine this being cute for a date night or a friend’s engagement party because it gives people something to do instead of just asking the same questions.
ranked_reactions.biggest_objection: A lot of relationship apps feel forced and corny to me, and I would not want an AI grading my relationship like a BuzzFeed quiz.
ranked_reactions.trigger_to_try: If friends used it for a party game and it was clearly playful, not pseudo-scientific, I would join in.
ranked_reactions.price_ceiling: Maybe a one-off $3-8 event pack, nothing recurring.
ranked_reactions.quote: Fun as a bit, not something I want hanging around on my phone.

ignored_or_rejected.reason: I do not have the pet-person content instinct for this, and even if I did, it feels like a novelty rabbit hole rather than something I would keep using.
This is basically irrelevant to my life unless I am selling something, and it also sounds a little too close to fake-native ad slop.

parsed.most_viral_artifact: A clean weekly workout recap card or short montage showing miles run, gym sessions, one honest voice-journal takeaway, and maybe a low-key milestone I can post to Instagram Stories or drop in the gym group chat.
parsed.trust_breaker: If it weaponizes missed goals into shamey notifications, invents emotional insights that feel manipulative, or auto-shares health or group-chat content without super clear consent, I would delete it immediately.
parsed.surprise: I am more willing to pay for reflection than motivation. Something that helps me make sense of my habits after the fact is actually more attractive to me than something that tries to push me harder in real time.

ranked_reactions.price_ceiling: Free is ideal here, or maybe a few dollars for a special occasion pack, but I would not subscribe.
ranked_reactions.quote: I would absolutely send a fake magazine cover from our family trip, but I am not paying monthly for that.

ranked_reactions.why_it_hits: I could see doing this once for an anniversary, date night, or with another couple because the game-show angle is cute and easy to share privately.
ranked_reactions.biggest_objection: This could get cringey really fast if the humor is off or if it invents personality insights that are way too confident.
ranked_reactions.trigger_to_try: A holiday or anniversary template that looked polished and private, not therapy-ish, would get me to try it.
ranked_reactions.price_ceiling: Maybe $5 to $10 one time for an occasion, not an ongoing subscription.
ranked_reactions.quote: This is fun if it stays playful and does not try to psychoanalyze my marriage.

ranked_reactions.why_it_hits: I do sometimes think better out loud, and if it could turn scattered voice notes into useful weekly themes or reminders, that is practical.
ranked_reactions.biggest_objection: I am very cautious about privacy here, and I do not want generic self-care advice dressed up as insight.
ranked_reactions.trigger_to_try: If it had a clear privacy promise and could summarize my week into a short action list instead of emotional fluff, I would test it.
ranked_reactions.price_ceiling: I would only pay a little, like under $8 a month, and only if it genuinely saved me time.
ranked_reactions.quote: If it can pull the actual useful stuff out of my rambling voice notes, that is interesting.

ranked_reactions.why_it_hits: I like practical learning tools more than abstract lessons, so travel or real-life scenario practice is the part that appeals to me.
ranked_reactions.biggest_objection: Putting me into visual selfie scenes sounds a little gimmicky, and if the pronunciation feedback is weak then the whole thing falls apart.
ranked_reactions.trigger_to_try: A specific vacation or travel pack with useful phrases and realistic voice practice would make me try it.
ranked_reactions.price_ceiling: Maybe a low monthly plan around $8 if it felt truly useful before a trip.
ranked_reactions.quote: I do not need cute AI scenes, I need to know what to say at a hotel desk without freezing.

ignored_or_rejected.reason: This feels built for people trying to feed TikTok constantly, and that is not me. I save memes, but I am not trying to produce motion remixes from them.
Even if it is funny once, it feels like too much maintenance for a joke. I would send one pet voice clip maybe, but I would not build a whole character universe.
This is just not relevant to my life stage, so I would scroll right past it.
I am a buyer, not a seller or creator brand. It immediately reads as work, not something for me.
Leaderboards and proof cards sound like pressure and performative productivity. I do not want one more app trying to turn my habits into content.

parsed.most_viral_artifact: A tasteful mini family recap video or photo-timeline from Family Lore Studio that I could send in the family text and maybe post to Instagram Stories on a holiday.
parsed.trust_breaker: If the app makes people look wrong, invents fake details, or pushes unrealistic polished outputs that I could never actually recreate in real life, I would delete it. Also, asking for broad access to private photos, chats, or voice notes without very clear control is a huge red flag for me.
parsed.surprise: I am much more willing to pay for AI when it helps me preserve family memories than when it helps me make social content. Sentimental usefulness beats virality for me.
